metadata.summary: Structured approach to identifying, defining, and documenting data needs before system development, producing data dictionaries and requirements documents that guide architects and developers.

content.title: Data Analysis
content.summary: A structured approach to identifying, defining, and documenting data needs before system development. Covers data requirements gathering, data element definition, conceptual/logical/physical modeling, data quality assessment across six dimensions, and data business rules. Produces a data dictionary and data requirements document that developers and architects use as authoritative data contracts.

why.p: Data exists in multiple systems with inconsistent definitions; reports show conflicting numbers; integration projects fail due to undiscovered schema mismatches. By mapping data entities, attributes, quality dimensions, and ownership before development, the BA prevents costly rework and ensures systems share a common data language.

list.item: Starting database or integration design and no shared data dictionary exists.
Reports from different systems show conflicting figures for the same metric.
Building ETL/integration layer between two or more systems.
Assessing data migration scope before cutover.
Data quality issues are causing operational problems and no baseline measurement exists.
Compliance requires documented data ownership and classification (GDPR, HIPAA).
Exploratory analytics spike where the data model will be thrown away — just query and iterate.
Event streaming architectures where schema-on-read is intentional; a rigid dictionary adds friction.
Frontend-only features with no new data persistence.
When an authoritative data dictionary already exists and is up to date — extend it, do not duplicate.
Pure UI/UX research where the output is a prototype, not a data model.
Greenfield prototypes pre-PMF where the schema changes weekly — formal data dictionaries become stale faster than written.
Statistical / exploratory data analysis (EDA, pandas, notebooks) — that is data science, not BA Data Analysis.
Pure ML feature engineering — features evolve through training pipelines, not through a BA-owned dictionary.
Tiny CRUD apps with under 10 entities — the ceremony costs more than it saves.
One-off ad-hoc reports where data lives only in a spreadsheet for a week.
High-trust regulated domains (HIPAA, PCI, SOX) where data classification MUST be human-signed — agents may draft, humans must approve.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Identify data needs: for each user role and system function, ask what information is displayed, captured, calculated, stored, and shared.
rule.rationale: Skipping need identification produces a data model that stores what developers guess rather than what the business requires.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Define data elements: document each attribute with name, business definition, type, format, valid values, source, and owner.
rule.rationale: Without business definitions, developers implement technical interpretations that conflict across systems and reports.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Create data model at three levels: conceptual (high-level entities and relationships for business stakeholders), logical (detailed attributes and rules for BA and developers), physical (tables, columns, indexes for database developers).
rule.rationale: Mixing levels produces models that are too abstract for developers or too technical for business review.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Analyze data quality across six dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness. Measure each with a quantitative metric before defining improvement targets.
rule.rationale: Unmeasured data quality produces subjective "bad data" complaints with no baseline to track improvement against.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Define data rules: document validation rules, derivation formulas, default values, constraints, and uniqueness requirements as testable statements.
rule.rationale: Business rules embedded in code without documentation become invisible and are lost when developers change; they must live in the data dictionary.

rule.statement: Measure accuracy as error rate (% of records with incorrect values vs. authoritative source), completeness as missing value %, consistency as conflict count across systems, timeliness as average data age, validity as % of records violating business rules, uniqueness as duplicate rate.
rule.rationale: Each dimension requires a distinct metric; a single "data quality score" hides which dimension is failing and which remediation to apply.

rule.statement: Assign each data quality dimension a numeric score (1-5), a traffic-light status (Green/Yellow/Red), and a remediation priority (H/M/L) in the assessment report.
rule.rationale: Qualitative assessments ("data is inconsistent") produce no remediation priority; numeric scores enable comparison across dimensions and tracking over time.

rule.statement: Never declare a dimension "Green" without a measured sample from production data of at least 100 records or 10% of total volume, whichever is larger.
rule.rationale: Small samples miss systemic issues; a 1% error rate on 10,000 records represents 100 corrupted records that will affect operations.

section.name: specific-use-cases-agentic
section.p: Specific agentic-automation use cases for data analysis:

list.item: Greenfield feature spec needs a data dictionary: agent reads the spec + existing schema, emits new entities + attributes with types, validations, and ownership before DDL is written.

Pre-migration data quality assessment: agent profiles a source table, fills the six-dimension scorecard (accuracy / completeness / consistency / timeliness / validity / uniqueness), and flags blockers per entity.
Cross-system definition reconciliation: agent diffs customer.email semantics across CRM, billing, and product DBs and produces a canonical definition + mapping table.
Conceptual → logical → physical drift check: agent compares data-dictionary.md against live schema (Postgres information_schema, dbt manifest) and opens issues per drift.
ERD generation from spec: agent writes Mermaid / dbml ERD from the entity table, keeps it in git, regenerates on every dictionary change.
Business-rule extraction from legacy code/SQL: agent reads stored procedures or app validators and reverse-engineers validation/derivation/constraint rule rows.
Data-volume sizing for capacity planning: agent reads dictionary + traffic assumptions and produces volumes table (current / 1yr / 3yr).

section.name: modeling-levels
section.p: Conceptual: entities and relationships only, no attributes. Audience: business stakeholders. Purpose: confirm scope.
Logical: entities with all attributes, data types, relationships with cardinality, business rules. Audience: BA, developers. Purpose: design contract.
Physical: tables, columns, indexes, foreign keys, partitioning. Audience: database developers. Purpose: implementation spec.

rule.statement: Document every business rule as one of: Validation (field-level constraint), Derivation (formula), Default (initial value), Constraint (cross-field or cross-entity), or Uniqueness (key definition).
rule.rationale: Mixing rule types in a single list makes it impossible to generate validators, ETL transformations, and database constraints from the same document.

list.item: Validation: Email must contain @ and a domain with at least one dot
Derivation: OrderTotal = sum(LineItem.Quantity * LineItem.Price) + ShippingCost - DiscountAmount
Default: CustomerStatus defaults to "Prospect" on record creation
Constraint: ShipDate must be on or after OrderDate
Uniqueness: CustomerID is system-generated and immutable; Email is unique across all active customers

example.title: Customer Entity Definition
example.code:
Entity: Customer Owner: CRM Team Source: Customer onboarding service
| Attribute | Type | Required | Validation | |--------------|------------|----------|-------------------------------------| | CustomerID | Integer | Yes | Auto-generated, immutable, unique | | FirstName | Text(50) | Yes | Not empty, no special characters | | LastName | Text(50) | Yes | Not empty | | Email | Text(100) | Yes | Valid email format, unique (active) | | Phone | Text(20) | No | Valid E.164 format if provided | | Status | Text(10) | Yes | Enum: Active, Inactive, Prospect | | CreatedDate | DateTime | Yes | Auto-set on creation, immutable |
Business Rules: DR-01: CustomerID is system-generated and immutable DR-02: Email must be unique across all active customers DR-03: Status defaults to "Prospect" for new customers DR-04: Inactive customers retain all records but cannot place orders

example.title: Data Quality Assessment Example
example.note: Product data assessment — shows how dimension scores drive prioritization.
example.code:
Assessment: Product Catalog Data (n=5,000 records, sampled 10%)
| Dimension | Score | Findings | Priority | |--------------|-------|---------------------------------------------------|----------| | Accuracy | 3/5 | 8% of prices incorrect vs. master catalog | High | | Completeness | 4/5 | 2% missing product descriptions | Medium | | Consistency | 2/5 | Product names differ between website and ERP | High | | Timeliness | 5/5 | Updated daily from supplier feed, acceptable | Low | | Validity | 4/5 | 1% records with invalid category codes | Medium | | Uniqueness | 3/5 | 5% duplicate SKUs from legacy import | High |
Priority issue: Consistency (score 2) — naming convention needed across all source systems before integration project proceeds. Recommendation: define canonical product name field in data dictionary and ETL transformation rules before cutover.

example.title: ER Notation Reference
example.code:
One customer has many orders: Customer 1 ----< N Order
One order has one or more line items (mandatory): Order 1 ----|| N LineItem
One product appears in zero or more line items (optional): Product 1 --o-< N LineItem
Symbols: 1 = exactly one N = many (one or more) o = zero (optional) | = mandatory < = crow's foot (many)

antipattern.description: No data dictionary — every developer defines data elements independently.
antipattern.reason: Without shared definitions, integration and reporting produce conflicting numbers that erode trust in all data.

antipattern.description: Skipping data quality assessment — assuming existing data is correct before migration.
antipattern.reason: Data quality issues discovered during migration cause project delays; assessing early allows remediation to run in parallel with development.

antipattern.description: No data ownership defined — no one accountable for data accuracy or access.
antipattern.reason: Unowned data degrades over time; ownership assigns the accountability needed to maintain quality rules.

antipattern.description: Ignoring historical data — modeling current state without considering how data changes over time.
antipattern.reason: Temporal requirements (audit trails, soft deletes, effective-date ranges) discovered after the physical model is built require schema rework.

antipattern.description: Definition drift between systems: The same entity (Customer, Order, Product) is defined differently across 4-7 systems.
antipattern.reason: The methodology offers no reconciliation pattern. Force a reconciliation step requiring both sources cited before merge.

antipattern.description: No lineage column: A data element without lineage (source system → transformations → consumers) is half-documented.
antipattern.reason: Agents and humans both omit it because the template does not require it. Lineage is mandatory; reject dictionaries lacking it.

antipattern.description: Static quality scorecard: Six dimensions are point-in-time; quality decays.
antipattern.reason: Without recurring re-assessment, scorecard is outdated within a quarter. Schedule weekly.

antipattern.description: PII / classification handled as afterthought: "Data Security" is one row at the bottom.
antipattern.reason: Classification (Public / Internal / Confidential / Restricted) drives storage, retention, masking, access. Make it first-class.

antipattern.description: Volume estimates without growth model: 1yr/3yr columns invite linear extrapolation.
antipattern.reason: Agents plug numbers without questioning the model. State the model explicitly (linear / exponential / seasonal).

antipattern.description: No event / state-transition modelling: Entities have lifecycles (Order: created → paid → shipped).
antipattern.reason: Methodology models attributes but not state machines. Add status enum + transitions table.

antipattern.description: Conflates relational with non-relational data: ERD assumes relational.
antipattern.reason: Document stores, event streams, and graph data need different primitives. Agents force everything into entity-attribute-relationship form.

antipattern.description: Business rules in prose: Rule examples are English sentences, not executable.
antipattern.reason: Agents cannot verify them mechanically without a DSL (JSON Schema, OPA Rego, dbt tests). Use executable rules.

section.name: agentic-prompt-patterns
section.p: Prompt pattern for dictionary draft from spec:
section.prompt:
You are a BA data modeller. Read the feature spec in <spec> and the canonical dictionary in <data-dictionary.md>. Output the diff: new entities, new attributes on existing entities, deprecated attributes.
For every attribute provide: name (PascalCase entity, snake_case attr), type, required (Y/N), valid values / range, validation rule (executable: regex, enum, FK), source system, owner (GitHub handle), classification (Public/Internal/Confidential/Restricted), lineage (source → transforms → consumers).
Reject any attribute lacking classification or owner. Output as the README's Data Dictionary table format plus a Mermaid ER diagram.

You profiled <table> with sample size <n>. Fill the six-dimension scorecard (accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness).
For each: state metric, finding, examples (redacted), score (1-5), priority (H/M/L). Use a 4-week rolling window for timeliness. For consistency, compare against <reference-system>.
No claim without a count + percentage. End with the top three remediation actions, each with owner and effort estimate.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive BA Data Analysis as a four-stage pipeline:

list.item: Extract — ingest the feature spec, existing dictionary, and live schema (information_schema, dbt manifest.json, Prisma schema.prisma).
Draft — produce or update the data dictionary with attributes, validations, classification, lineage, and Mermaid/dbml ERD.
Validate — run quality probes against sample data (Great Expectations / dbt tests / Soda checks) and fill the six-dimension scorecard.
Govern — diff against canonical dictionary, open PRs per drift, request human sign-off for any classification change.

section.p: Persist data-dictionary.md, erd.mmd, and quality-scorecard.md in git so every run is a reviewable diff.

rule.statement: Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (converts approved changes into SDD tasks), password-scrubber-agent (mandatory pre-step; SOPs contain credentials), a purpose-built data-modeller agent (canonical dictionary + classification rules; output JSON Schema for codegen), a schema-drift agent (cron, daily; diffs information_schema against data-dictionary.md), a data-quality agent (cron, weekly; runs quality suites and posts scorecard).

section.name: cli-tools-and-services

list.item: dbt (data build tool) — Logical/physical model + executable tests. (https://docs.getdbt.com)
dbt-osmosis — Auto-propagates column descriptions and tests across dbt models.
great_expectations — Data quality suites mapping to six dimensions. (https://docs.greatexpectations.io)
soda-core — YAML-defined quality checks runnable in CI. (https://docs.soda.io)
sqlfluff — Lint dictionary-bound SQL before agents run it. (https://docs.sqlfluff.com)
schemaspy — Generates HTML + ERDs from any JDBC database. (https://schemaspy.org)
tbls — CI-friendly DB documentation (markdown + Mermaid ERD) from live schema. (https://github.com/k1LoW/tbls)
dbml-cli — Render ERDs from .dbml; round-trips to SQL. (https://dbml.dbdiagram.io/cli)
mermaid-cli — Render erDiagram Mermaid blocks to SVG/PNG in CI.
datacontract-cli — Lint and verify Data Contract YAML. (https://cli.datacontract.com)
sqlglot — Parse + transpile SQL, extract column lineage. (https://github.com/tobymao/sqlglot)
openlineage / marquez — Capture column-level lineage from dbt, Airflow, Spark. (https://openlineage.io)
presidio (Microsoft) — PII detection/redaction before LLM ingestion. (https://microsoft.github.io/presidio)
liquibase / flyway / alembic — Migrations as the executable physical model.

section.p: Platforms and services for data catalogs and governance:

list.item: dbt Cloud / dbt Core — SaaS / OSS. Canonical contract layer; agents read manifest.json for typed model graph.
dbdiagram.io / dbdocs.io — SaaS. .dbml is plain text, git-versionable. Best-fit for reviewable ERD in PR.
DataHub — OSS (LinkedIn). Catalog + lineage; agents push/pull definitions, classifications. (GraphQL + REST)
OpenMetadata — OSS. Catalog with column-level lineage and quality test integration. (REST + Python SDK)
Atlan / Alation / Collibra — SaaS. Enterprise catalogs; SSO + governance gates often slow agent loops.
Monte Carlo / Bigeye / Anomalo — SaaS. Observability layer feeding timeliness dimension automatically.
Soda Cloud — SaaS. Pairs with soda-core for quality reports.
Great Expectations Cloud — SaaS. Hosted GX with API.
Postgres / MySQL / SQL Server / Snowflake / BigQuery — DB. Agents introspect live schema via information_schema.
Apache Atlas — OSS. Heavyweight; pick only if already running Hadoop ecosystem.

rule.statement: Classification is mandatory, not optional: Every attribute carries Public / Internal / Confidential / Restricted; downstream tooling keys off it.
Owner is a human handle, not a team: GitHub username or email; one per attribute.
Lineage column on every attribute: source_system → transforms → consumers. Without it the dictionary is a graveyard.
Executable rules, not English: Validation expressed as regex, enum, FK, or dbt/GX test ID. Prose rules drift; tests fail loudly.
Naming is an ADR: Pick snake_case columns + PascalCase entities + singular table names and write it down once.
Conceptual model first, in Mermaid, in git: Stakeholder review happens in PR comments, not Visio.
Reconcile before extending: When adding customer.email, search every system for existing email and document mapping.
Quality scorecard is recurring, not point-in-time: Schedule weekly; track trend, not snapshot.
State machines for entities with lifecycles: Add status enum + transitions table; reject transitions outside table.
Retention + DPA columns for Confidential / Restricted: Tie to records-of-processing-activity (GDPR Article 30).
Volumes assume explicit growth model: Linear / exponential / seasonal; cite assumption next to number.
Definitions are versioned: Definition change is semantic-versioned PR with deprecation notice; consumers get migration time.

antipattern.description: Hallucinated columns: Agents invent created_at / updated_at columns that live schema does not have.
antipattern.reason: Always pass introspected schema in prompt; reject any column not present without explicit ADD task.

antipattern.description: Type-coercion blind spots: Agents map varchar(50) to text interchangeably.
antipattern.reason: In some warehouses (BigQuery STRING vs SQL Server NVARCHAR) the difference matters. Pin types to target dialect.

antipattern.description: Silent classification downgrade: Agent re-emits attribute and drops Confidential tag.
antipattern.reason: Always include classification of every existing attribute in prompt; reject diffs lowering classification without ADR.

antipattern.description: Definition collision: Two systems both define Customer; agent picks one and overwrites.
antipattern.reason: Force reconciliation step requiring both sources cited before merge.

antipattern.description: PII leakage in sample rows: "Show me 5 example rows" → real emails in prompt.
antipattern.reason: Always pre-redact via Presidio or use synthetic samples generated from schema.

antipattern.description: Volume math fabrication: Agents produce confident 3-year numbers from single multiplier.
antipattern.reason: Require statement of model (linear / exp / seasonal) + assumption inputs; otherwise "needs human estimate".

antipattern.description: Cardinality misnaming: 1:N vs N:M confusion creates wrong ERDs.
antipattern.reason: Constrain agent to derive cardinality from FK + uniqueness constraints in information_schema, never prose.

antipattern.description: Dropped historical context: Agents rewrite description, losing rationale.
antipattern.reason: Always require change-log row appended, never a description replacement.

antipattern.description: No nullability invariants: Agents flag Required: Y but emit no NOT NULL constraint.
antipattern.reason: Bind the dictionary's Required column to migration generator (alembic / liquibase) so they cannot diverge.

antipattern.description: Inferring meaning from name: is_active differs in CRM vs billing.
antipattern.reason: Force lineage + business definition on every Boolean.

antipattern.description: Token bombs from full schema dumps: 400-table catalog blows context window.
antipattern.reason: Slice by feature scope; pass only entities the spec touches.

antipattern.description: Dictionary becomes only source of truth, then rots: Agent updates dictionary but not migration.
antipattern.reason: Always pair dictionary PRs with migration + dbt tests in same commit, gated by tbls/schemaspy diff.

antipattern.description: Mermaid ERD limits: Diagrams over 30 entities become unreadable.
antipattern.reason: Force per-bounded-context diagrams (one Mermaid file per domain).

template.purpose: Per-entity template with attributes table, example records, relationships, and business rules.
Project-level data requirements: entities, attributes, derived data, quality thresholds, volumes, integration, security.
Assessment report per quality dimension with scoring table and recommendations.
